summary:
The Qujialing culture was a Neolithic culture of the middle Yangtze River region in present-day Hubei and Hunan provinces, China, flourishing approximately 3300–2600 BCE. It is characterized by distinctive black-painted and orange-painted pottery, including fine eggshell-thin cups and vessels decorated with geometric motifs. Qujialing settlements were relatively large and included evidence of rice cultivation and pig domestication, reflecting a mature agricultural society.

The culture shows interactions with contemporaneous cultures to the east, including the [[event:1674|Liangzhu culture]], through shared pottery styles and exchange of prestige goods. Qujialing sites have yielded evidence of early silk weaving and spindle whorls, suggesting textile production was an important economic activity. The culture was eventually succeeded by the Shijiahe culture, which developed out of the Qujialing tradition in the same region around 2600 BCE.
